data.title: Iran's path to de-dollarization: When weapons begin to be settled with cryptocurrencies
data.description: When a country begins accepting cryptocurrency payments for arms orders, crypto assets are no longer simply a matter of "financial innovation" or a "gray market tool," but are formally incorporated into the national survival and international power dynamics. In January 2026, Iran's Ministry of Defense Export Center, Mindex, explicitly stated in official documents that its overseas military contracts could accept cryptocurrency, barter trade, or Iranian rials as payment methods. Arms trade has always been one of the most sanctioned, regulated, and sensitive cross-border transaction scenarios. Iran's decision to publicly include cryptocurrency as a payment option in this area signifies one thing: crypto assets are being systematically used by Iran as a "financial tool to resist sanctions." Driven by Realistic Constraints: Over the past few years, Iran has been under three highly realistic constraints: The local currency, the rial, has been depreciating for a long time, and the foreign exchange system is fragile. The international banking system has been largely cut off. Energy exports and military trade continue to face settlement and delivery risks. Against this backdrop, in 2025, Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf publicly stated that without accepting cryptocurrencies, Iran would be unable to achieve its national goal of a 10% digital economy in GDP, and called for the prompt development of a national roadmap for crypto assets. This isn't technological idealism; it's a sober assessment formed under the reality of long-term sanctions—without the introduction of encryption, many economic goals simply cannot be achieved. The world's fourth-largest cryptocurrency mining center. In reality, Iran's dependence on crypto assets is far more radical than its statements suggest. On the one hand, Iran has become the world's fourth-largest cryptocurrency mining center. Thanks to substantial electricity subsidies, even rampant illegal mining has brought it considerable computing power and crypto assets. On the other hand, crypto assets are also deeply embedded in more sensitive areas. Israel's National Counter-Terrorism Financing Authority disclosed that addresses associated with the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) have received approximately $1.5 billion in USDT. While some addresses may belong to exchanges or sharing services, the sheer scale of this number is enough to illustrate that stablecoins are becoming a crucial liquidity vehicle for Iran to circumvent sanctions. A "Firefly" in the Dark In January 2026, due to protests and a currency crisis, Iran implemented a nationwide internet shutdown. This should have been a "fatal blow" to cryptocurrency trading, but the outcome was unexpected. In the absence of internet access, various offline or weak network solutions were quickly discussed and deployed: Starlink satellite network; Blockstream satellite network supporting global broadcasting of Bitcoin data; Bitchat Bluetooth mesh communication tool; Darkwire, a Bitcoin transmission solution without internet access; and Machankura, which supports sending and receiving Bitcoin over telecommunications networks. These solutions are not mature and cannot replace the internet on a large scale, but in such an extreme environment, the crypto industry has demonstrated its remarkable resilience. When traditional communication and financial systems fail simultaneously, crypto assets become a "last resort." The era of "strategic tools" is exemplified by Iran's experience, a microcosm of how nations survive under extreme sanctions. It demonstrates the unique geopolitical value of cryptocurrencies: bypassing traditional financial systems to transfer value and acquire strategic resources. Russia's oil trade, Venezuela's "shadow Bitcoin reserves," and now Iran's arms deals all point to an undeniable reality: cryptocurrencies are evolving from "financial tools" to "geopolitical tools," becoming a new medium connecting national strategies with the global economy.

data.title: Will the US economy rebound like a spring in 2026?
data.description:
Author: BiyaNews

Recently, Cathie Wood, the "Tech Queen" of ARK Invest, released her annual heavyweight outlook letter. While Wall Street is generally filled with concerns about a "rolling recession" and high valuations, Wood paints a completely different picture: the US economy is not at its limit, but rather a "spring compressed to its limit," poised for a V-shaped rebound driven by technological deflation and a productivity explosion in 2026. She even boldly predicts that inflation will not only subside but may even turn negative.

This sounds counterintuitive, right? After all, we've just gone through a rate hike cycle, and market sentiment has been volatile. But Wood's logic is very clear, and she's betting on disruptive technologies that are converging and could reshape everything. Today, we'll dissect this "map of the future" to see what opportunities and risks it holds.

The Tightened "Economic Spring": The Recession is Only Localized. Wood's core argument is that the economy of the past few years was not a widespread overheating, but rather a "rolling recession." The Federal Reserve's aggressive interest rate hikes, like a precise scalpel, suppressed specific sectors, while other parts accumulated enormous rebound potential. Consider the data: Existing home sales have plummeted by about 41% from their 2021 peak and have lingered at low levels for two years, a slump comparable to the early 1980s—when the US population was only 35% of what it is now. The manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) has struggled in contraction territory (below 50) for nearly three years. Meanwhile, consumer confidence among low- and middle-income groups has fallen to multi-decade lows, and recently even confidence among high-income groups has begun to collapse.

Doesn't this resemble a spring being compressed to varying degrees in different parts? Housing, manufacturing, and parts of the consumer sector bore the brunt of the pressure. But on the other hand, a key indicator—spending on non-defense capital goods (excluding aircraft)—after a two-decade-long plateau following the tech bubble, has finally broken through its ceiling, driven by new technologies such as artificial intelligence.

... Wood believes we are standing at the beginning of the strongest capital expenditure cycle in history, and "an AI bubble? She says it will take several more years to form."

Three major "decompression" drivers: the combined effect of policy, inflation, and technology

For a spring to bounce back, it needs to release pressure. Wood points out three decompression valves that will soon work together:

  1. Fiscal and regulatory "loosening": She expects deregulation (especially in the AI ​​and crypto asset sectors) and potential tax cuts to unleash innovation.

  2. Fiscal and regulatory "loosening": She anticipates that deregulation (especially in the AI ​​and crypto asset sectors) and potential tax cuts will unleash innovation.

  3. For example, tax incentives such as accelerated depreciation could push the effective tax rate for US companies to one of the lowest tiers globally, directly benefiting cash flow and capital expenditure. The "Unexpected Downturn" in Inflation: Here her view is the boldest. She believes that inflation may fall to extremely low levels, or even turn negative, in the coming years. Supporting arguments include: oil prices have fallen sharply from their highs; the growth of new and existing home prices has slowed significantly (builders are lowering prices to clear inventory); and most importantly, productivity is improving. In the third quarter of last year, US non-farm productivity increased by 1.9% year-on-year, exceeding the growth in hourly wages, leading to a decrease in the unit labor cost inflation rate to 1.2%. Technology is becoming the most powerful deflationary force.

  1. Technology-driven "productivity explosion": This is the cornerstone of all of Wood's predictions. She believes that the convergence of five major innovation platforms—artificial intelligence, robotics, energy storage, blockchain, and multi-omics sequencing—will drive productivity growth to an annualized rate of 4-6% in the coming years. This will not only suppress inflation but also create enormous wealth. A striking statistic is that, according to some benchmarks, AI inference costs are declining at a rate of up to 99% per year. The collapse of the cost curve will trigger explosive growth in applications.

  1. Technology-driven "productivity explosion": This is the cornerstone of all of Wood's predictions. She believes that the convergence of five major innovation platforms—artificial intelligence, robotics, energy storage, blockchain, and multi-omics sequencing—will drive productivity growth to an annualized rate of 4-6% in the coming years. This will not only suppress inflation but also create enormous wealth. The Chain Reaction of Asset Allocation: Gold, Bitcoin, and the US Dollar If the above macroeconomic projection holds true, then the performance logic of various assets will be rewritten. Wood also offered her insights in her letter: Gold vs. Bitcoin: Last year, gold prices surged while Bitcoin remained relatively weak. An interesting interpretation is "relative scarcity." The rate at which global stock market wealth (measured by the MSCI World Index) is created may exceed the growth rate of gold supply (approximately 1.8% annualized). However, the key is that gold miners can increase production in response to high prices, while Bitcoin's supply is pre-set and completely unmanageable. From an asset allocation perspective, Bitcoin has extremely low correlation with gold and other major asset classes, making it an excellent diversification tool. Will the US Dollar Make a Comeback? Last year, the US dollar index experienced a significant decline, and voices predicting the "end of American exceptionalism" were heard throughout the market. But Wood believes that if her prediction comes true—that the US achieves higher returns on invested capital through technological breakthroughs and policy adjustments—then the dollar will regain its upward trend. She draws a parallel to the near doubling of the dollar in the early days of Reaganomics in the 1980s. Where are the "returns" on AI investment? Current investments in AI infrastructure such as data centers are booming, far exceeding the norm of the past decade. A crucial question is: where will the returns from this massive investment be reflected? Wood points out that, besides hardware giants like Nvidia and cloud service providers, the real growth dividends are flowing to unlisted AI-native companies. Companies like OpenAI and Anthropic are seeing revenues grow at an astonishing rate (tens or even hundreds of times). The future leaders in AI will be those companies that can translate cutting-edge technologies into tangible applications. High Valuations in the Stock Market: Opportunity or Trap? The current valuations of US stocks are undeniably at historical highs, deterring many investors. Wood acknowledges this, but offers a historical perspective: the strongest bull markets in history have often been accompanied by a "compression" of the price-to-earnings ratio (P/E). For example, in the bull markets of the mid-1990s and mid-2000s, the S&P 500 achieved substantial annualized returns, but the P/E ratio declined during the same period. The core driver was that corporate earnings growth outpaced stock price increases. She believes that if productivity-driven real GDP growth accelerates while inflation slows, the current cycle may follow a similar pattern—strong earnings growth digesting or even lowering valuation multiples. This might mean that market opportunities will shift from simple valuation expansion to a deeper exploration of genuine earnings growth and industry disruptors. Cathy Wood's outlook is, as always, bold and systematic. Her predictions are built on the core assumptions of "technological deflation" and a "productivity revolution." If she's right, we may be standing at the beginning of a new era, where growth and investment logic will be reshaped; if she's wrong, the compressed "spring" may lose its elasticity due to other shocks. Regardless, the value of this deduction lies in forcing us to consider long-term forces that transcend short-term fluctuations: How much efficiency can AI actually improve? How can policy resonate with innovation? In an era of collapsing technology cost curves, what assets can truly preserve value? As investors, we need to maintain an open mind, neither blindly optimistic about high valuations nor ignoring the power of disruptive technologies. Markets are always changing, but understanding the underlying logic of change is the best way to cope with uncertainty.

data.title: Iran’s Crypto Boom Swells to $7.8B As Protests Amidst Economic Crisis And Protest
data.description:
Iran Crypto's Popularity Spikes Amid Unrest, Inflation, and Internet Shutdown

Iran’s cryptocurrency ecosystem surged to $7.78 billion in 2025, accelerating sharply during weeks of nationwide protests sparked by economic collapse and political repression, according to new data from blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis.

🇮🇷Iran recorded $7.8 billion in crypto activity as inflation, economic stress, and internet shutdowns pushed people to alternatives.

A large part of the inflow came from state-linked actors facing sanctions. pic.twitter.com/2fLB8TngPT

— Bitinning (@bitinning) January 16, 2026

The spike coincided with mass demonstrations that erupted in late December after the Iranian rial plunged to record lows against the U.S. dollar, deepening an already severe cost-of-living crisis. As unrest spread, authorities responded with internet shutdowns and forceful crackdowns, pushing many Iranians to seek alternatives to the traditional financial system.

In that environment, Bitcoin emerged as both a store of value and an escape hatch, offering liquidity and portability at a time when banks, currency controls and state oversight became liabilities rather than safeguards.

Chainalysis found a pronounced increase in daily crypto transfers and transaction volumes during the unrest, with the most revealing signal coming from exchange activity. Large numbers of users withdrew Bitcoin from Iranian exchanges into unattributed personal wallets, a move analysts interpret as a rush toward self-custody.

In the last 12 hours, Iran has experienced significant civil unrest, with a total of 385 reports, including 264 protests and 8 clashes. The protests are largely driven by public discontent and have been met with a notable police presence, particularly in urban areas. The internet… pic.twitter.com/Y8ELobIEsR

— nkl (@NavidKianiL) January 16, 2026

Chainalysis said, noting that Iranians were taking direct possession of Bitcoin at a far higher rate during protests than before

“Most telling is the surge in withdrawals from Iranian exchanges to personal Bitcoin wallets.”

The behavior, the firm added, reflects a rational response to the collapse of the rial, which has lost so much value that it is now effectively unusable against major global currencies.

Experts say this shift highlights growing distrust in centralized institutions during periods of crisis. With capital controls tightening and inflation eroding savings, Bitcoin’s fixed supply and censorship-resistant design have made it an increasingly attractive hedge against both monetary debasement and political risk.

Chainalysis reports a surge in Bitcoin buying in Iran as the rial rapidly loses value.

After falling from 42 to 1,050 per USD in a matter of weeks, the currency has effectively collapsed, pushing residents to use #BTC to preserve savings and move funds abroad outside the banking… pic.twitter.com/1viSgyTuwk

— BTC Live (@btcliveco) January 16, 2026

Crypto as resistance — and a regime tool

While ordinary Iranians are turning to crypto to preserve wealth and bypass restrictions, Chainalysis also observed a striking paradox: the Iranian state is doing the same.

Addresses linked to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) accounted for roughly half of Iran’s total crypto activity in the fourth quarter of 2025, with more than $3 billion received over the year. The data underscores how digital assets have become embedded on both sides of Iran’s economic divide — used by citizens seeking autonomy and by state-linked actors navigating sanctions and isolation.

According to Chainalysis, Bitcoin’s appeal in this context goes beyond price appreciation. Its self-custodial and censorship-resistant nature offers flexibility in environments where traditional assets can be frozen, seized or rendered inaccessible overnight.

Iran is not an outlier. Chainalysis noted that regions facing war, economic collapse or government crackdowns consistently show similar spikes in Bitcoin withdrawals during periods of instability.

Despite a blanket ban, protesters in Nepal are using crypto to dodge bank restrictions and surveillance pic.twitter.com/ktwBZ9VGRn

— Neeraj K. Agrawal (@NeerajKA) September 12, 2025

In Iran’s case, the trend is reinforced by scale. TRM Labs estimates that roughly $3.7 billion in crypto flows moved through the country between January and July 2025, while data from Statista suggests that around seven million Iranians — nearly 8% of the population — are now crypto users. Persistent sanctions pressure and limited access to global financial rails have only accelerated adoption.

Crypto’s role in a fractured economy

As sanctions intensify and the rial continues its downward spiral, analysts believe cryptocurrency will remain a critical tool for financial sovereignty in Iran. Bitcoin’s liquidity, portability and independence from government control make it uniquely suited for populations facing systemic instability — especially when individuals may need to move funds quickly or operate outside official channels.

Chainalysis concluded that crypto’s role in Iran is unlikely to fade once the protests subside. Instead, digital assets appear increasingly woven into the country’s economic reality, serving as both a hedge against collapse and a parallel financial system in an environment where trust in traditional structures has eroded.

data.title: From Cash to Crypto to Prison: Utah Man Sentenced for Multi-Million Dollar Fraud
data.description:
Operating from a quiet corner of Washington County, Brian Garry Sewell appeared to be a high-flying expert in the digital gold rush.

To his students and investors, he was a dual-degree scholar from Stanford and Johns Hopkins with the Midas touch for cryptocurrency.

In reality, the 54-year-old was a GED holder weaving a multi-million-pound web of lies that has now culminated in a three-year federal prison sentence.

The judgment, handed down on 15 January 2026, marks the end of a sprawling enforcement effort that saw the Department of Justice pursue Sewell for both blatant investment fraud and the shadowy operation of an unlicensed money-moving business.

Southern Utah Man Sentenced to Three Years in Prison for Wire Fraud that Cost Investors Millions of Dollars

PRESS RELEASE: 🔗https://t.co/gWgh0bA0W0pic.twitter.com/ImDRq7i2rM

— US Attorney Utah (@USAO_UT) January 15, 2026

The Fiction Of A High Yield Crypto Hedge Fund

Between December 2017 and April 2024, Sewell orchestrated a scheme that preyed on the aspirations of 17 individual investors.

He claimed to run a sophisticated hedge fund, the Rockwell Fund, which he promised would use advanced strategies to deliver massive returns with negligible risk.

To build trust, he fabricated an elite educational background and a history of successful fund management.

The deception worked, allowing Sewell to amass more than $2.9 million in investor capital.

📰 Breaking News: Crypto teacher's costly lesson – $1.2M student investment gone in fake hedge fund scam! 🚨 Beware !!!#CryptoScams#InvestmentWarning#CryptoFraud"https://t.co/3Tq8AxeCaC

— CryptoIdea (@cryptoideatoken) February 3, 2024

However, the promised trading never happened.

Instead of managing a high-tech fund, Sewell simply held the funds in Bitcoin.

The scheme took a final, disastrous turn when Sewell claimed his digital wallet was hacked and the assets looted, leaving his victims with nothing.

Robert Bohls, Special Agent in Charge of the Salt Lake City FBI, said in an official statement,

"Sewell preyed on his victims by lying about his experience and promising returns he could not deliver, leaving individuals and families to bear the consequences of his deception.”

Processing Millions For The Criminal Underworld

While the investment fraud was unfolding, Sewell was also running a parallel "cash-to-crypto" operation through his company, Rockwell Capital Management.

From March to September 2020, he acted as a high-volume, unlicensed money transmitter, converting bulk cash into digital assets for a fee.

Source: Linkedin

Federal prosecutors revealed that this business moved more than $5.4 million (£4 million) for third-party clients, including individuals involved in drug trafficking and large-scale fraud.

By ignoring mandatory anti-money laundering (AML) registrations and reporting requirements, Sewell provided a critical service for criminals looking to disguise the origins of their illicit wealth.

Federal Authorities Signal No Safe Harbour

The dual cases against Sewell resulted in a total restitution order of $3,822,909.

This includes over $3.6 million for defrauded investors, a mortgage lender, and a credit union, along with $217,727 destined for the U.S. Department of Homeland Security.

His two three-year prison terms will run concurrently, followed by 36 months of supervised release.

Legal experts suggest that this prosecution in Utah indicates a broader strategy by the Department of Justice to target regional operators, not just the industry's biggest names.

By using unlicensed money transmission charges as a "fail-safe," prosecutors can secure convictions based on the illegality of the operation itself, regardless of whether a jury finds specific fraudulent intent.

A Record Year For Global Crypto Crime

Sewell’s sentencing arrives as the scale of digital asset misuse reaches unprecedented heights.

According to data from Chainalysis, 2025 was the most volatile year on record for illicit activity, with criminal addresses receiving a staggering $154 billion (£114 billion).

This represents a 162% surge compared to the revised $57.2 billion reported in 2024.

In the introductory chapter to our 2026 Crypto Crime Report, we reveal that illicit cryptocurrency transactions received at least $154 billion in 2025 (a 162% YoY increase). Nation-state activity and sanctions evasion drove the surge, marking a new phase in crypto crime. Read… pic.twitter.com/gedfxuDgUs

— Chainalysis (@chainalysis) January 8, 2026

The rise is largely attributed to the professionalisation of on-chain infrastructure, where illicit organisations now provide "laundering-as-a-service" to transnational networks.

As authorities increase their focus on both large exchanges and individual "cash-out" hubs like Sewell’s, the gap between the regulated financial world and the digital underground continues to shrink.

data.title: Why did a bill lead Coinbase to decide to antagonize the entire industry?
data.description: On January 14th, the CLARITY Act, a bill aimed at regulating the US crypto market, is about to face a crucial vote in the Senate Banking Committee. On the eve of this industry-breaking moment, Coinbase founder and CEO Brian Armstrong announced that the company would completely withdraw its support for the bill, arguing that "a bad bill is worse than no bill at all." This news immediately sent shockwaves through the industry. But what was truly surprising was that almost all other major players in the industry stood against Coinbase. Chris Dixon, a partner at venture capital giant a16z, believes "now is the time to move forward"; Brad Garlinghouse, CEO of payment giant Ripple, stated that "clarity is better than chaos"; Arjun Sethi, co-CEO of rival exchange Kraken, bluntly stated that "this is a test of political will"; even Coin Center, a non-profit organization known for defending decentralized principles, said the bill is "fundamentally correct in terms of developer protection." On one side is the undisputed industry leader, and on the other is its former key ally. This is no longer the old story of the crypto industry fighting against Washington regulators, but a civil war within the industry. The Isolated Coinbase: Why is Coinbase isolated by others? The answer is simple: almost all other major players, based on their own business interests and survival philosophies, judged that this imperfect bill was the best option at present. First, there's a16z. As Silicon Valley's most prestigious crypto investment firm, a16z's portfolio spans almost every crypto sector. For them, the most fatal flaw isn't the stringency of any specific provision, but the ongoing regulatory uncertainty. A clear legal framework, even with its flaws, can provide fertile ground for the entire ecosystem they invest in. Chris Dixon's position represents the consensus among investors; in their eyes, regulatory certainty is more important than a perfect bill. Second, there's the exchange Kraken. As one of Coinbase's most direct competitors, Kraken is actively preparing for its IPO. A regulatory endorsement from Congress will greatly enhance its valuation in the public market. In contrast, the restrictions on stablecoin yields in the bill have a far smaller financial impact on Kraken than on Coinbase. Trading controllable short-term business losses for the long-term, substantial benefits of an IPO is a no-brainer for Kraken. Now consider payment giant Ripple. Its CEO, Brad Garlinghouse, summarized his stance in six words: "clarity beats chaos." Behind this lies Ripple's years-long, multi-million dollar legal battle with the SEC. For a company exhausted by regulation, any form of peace is a victory. Even an imperfect bill is far better than endless court battles. Finally, there's the advocacy organization Coin Center. As a non-profit, their stance is least driven by commercial interests. Their core demand for years has been to ensure that software developers are not wrongly categorized as "money transmitters" and subjected to excessive regulation. This bill fully incorporates their advocated Blockchain Regulatory Certainty Act (BRCA), legally protecting developers. With the core objective achieved, other details can be compromised. Their support represents the endorsement of the industry's "fundamentals." When venture capitalists, exchanges, payment companies, and advocacy organizations are all on the same side, Coinbase's stance stands out starkly. So the question arises: if the entire industry sees a way forward, what exactly does Coinbase see that would cause it to risk industry division to block it all? Business Model Determines Stance The answer lies in Coinbase's financial statements—a $1.4 billion hole. To understand Armstrong's actions, one must first understand Coinbase's existential anxiety. For a long time, a large portion of Coinbase's revenue has relied on cryptocurrency transaction fees. The vulnerability of this model was exposed during the crypto winter; it profited immensely during bull markets but suffered sharp revenue declines and even quarterly losses during bear markets. The company had to find new, more stable revenue sources. Stablecoin yields are the second growth curve that Coinbase has found. Its business model is not complicated. Users hold USDC, a stablecoin pegged 1:1 to the US dollar, on the Coinbase platform. Coinbase then lends out these funds through DeFi protocols (such as Morpho), earning interest and returning a portion of the profits to users as rewards. According to data on Coinbase's official website, ordinary users can earn an annualized return of 3.5%, while paid members can achieve a return of up to 4.5%. According to Coinbase's Q3 2025 financial report, its "interest and funding income" reached $355 million, the vast majority of which came from its stablecoin business. Based on this, it is estimated that this business contributed approximately $1.4 billion in revenue to Coinbase in 2025, representing an increasingly larger proportion of its total revenue. In a bear market with sluggish trading volume, this stable and substantial cash flow is Coinbase's lifeline. A new provision in the CLARITY Act strikes at Coinbase's Achilles' heel. This provision stipulates that stablecoin issuers or affiliates cannot pay yields for users' "static holdings," but are allowed to pay yields for "activities and transactions." This means that simply storing USDC in a Coinbase account to earn interest will be prohibited. This is a fatal blow to Coinbase; if the bill passes, this $1.4 billion in revenue could shrink significantly or even disappear. Furthermore, the issues Armstrong listed on social media seem more like a struggle at the market structure level: the draft bill would effectively block the path to tokenized stocks/securities, setting higher barriers for DeFi, making it easier for regulators to access user financial data, and weakening the CFTC's role in the spot market. The stablecoin yield ban is just the most direct and immediate blow to Coinbase. Different interests lead to different choices. Kraken's stablecoin business is far smaller than Coinbase's, allowing it to trade short-term losses for the long-term value of its IPO; Ripple's core is payments, where regulatory clarity is paramount; a16z's strategy revolves around the entire ecosystem, where the gains or losses of individual projects are insignificant. Coinbase sees a precipice, while other companies see a bridge. However, this game also involves a third party: traditional banking. The American Bankers Association (ABA) and the Banking Policy Institute (BPI) believe that allowing stablecoins to pay yields will lead to trillions of dollars in deposits flowing out of the traditional banking system, posing an existential threat to thousands of community banks. Back in July 2025, the Stablecoin Genius Act was passed, explicitly allowing "third parties and affiliates" of stablecoins to pay yields, leaving legal room for Coinbase's model. However, in the seven months that followed, the banking industry launched a powerful lobbying campaign, ultimately succeeding in adding a "static holding" ban to the Clarity Act. Banks weren't afraid of the 3.5% yield, but rather of losing their deposit pricing power. When users can freely choose whether to deposit their funds in banks or crypto platforms, the banks' decades-long low-interest monopoly will end—this is the essence of the conflict. So, faced with such a complex game of interests, why did only Armstrong choose the most decisive approach? Two Survival Philosophies This is not merely a conflict of commercial interests, but a clash of two fundamentally different survival philosophies. One is Silicon Valley-style idealism and uncompromising stance; the other is Washington-style pragmatism and gradual improvement. Brian Armstrong represents the former. This isn't the first time he's publicly clashed with regulators. Back in 2023, when the SEC sued Coinbase for illegally operating a securities exchange, Armstrong publicly criticized the SEC for its "inconsistent stance," revealing that Coinbase had held over 30 meetings with regulators, repeatedly requesting clear rules but receiving no response. His stance has been consistent: he supports regulation, but resolutely opposes "bad regulation." In his view, accepting a fundamentally flawed bill is more dangerous than having no bill at all. Because once a law is enacted, future amendments become incredibly difficult. Accepting a bill that stifles a core business model for short-term certainty is tantamount to drinking poison to quench thirst. Armstrong's logic is that they can fight at all costs now, even if it's painful, because it preserves the possibility of fighting for better rules in the future. Compromise now would mean permanently giving up the stablecoin yield front. In this war for the company's future, compromise is surrender. Other leaders in the crypto industry, however, demonstrate a completely different pragmatic philosophy. They are well-versed in Washington's rules of the game: legislation is the art of compromise, and perfection is the enemy of excellence. Kraken's CEO, Ceci, believes it's important to first establish a legal framework that gives the industry legitimate social status, and then gradually improve it in practice through continuous lobbying and participation. Survival first, development later. Ripple's CEO, Garlinghouse, places certainty above all else. Years of litigation have taught him that struggling in the legal quagmire is a huge drain on a company. An imperfect peace is far better than a perfect war. a16z's Dixon, from a strategic perspective of global competition, believes that if the US delays legislation due to internal disputes, it will only hand over its central position in global financial innovation to Singapore, Dubai, or Hong Kong. Armstrong is still fighting Washington's battles with Silicon Valley methods, while others have learned Washington's language. One is the principle of "better to die a glorious death than live a shameful life," the other is the pragmatic consideration of "as long as the green hills remain, there will always be firewood." Which is wiser? No one can say for sure until time gives us the answer. But what is certain is that both choices come at a heavy price. The Cost of Civil War What is the real cost of this civil war ignited by Coinbase? First, it has caused a political split within the crypto industry. According to Politico, Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott's decision to postpone the vote came after Coinbase's last-minute defection and when the bill's support among bipartisan lawmakers was uncertain. While Coinbase's actions weren't the sole reason, they were undoubtedly a key factor pushing the entire effort into chaos. If the bill ultimately fails as a result, other companies might blame Coinbase, arguing that it hindered the industry's progress for its own self-interest. More seriously, this public infighting significantly weakens the crypto industry's collective bargaining power in Washington. When lawmakers see the industry unable to form a unified voice, they will feel confused and frustrated. A divided industry is vulnerable to powerful traditional financial lobbying groups. Secondly, it exposes the regulatory dilemma of the digital age. The CLARITY Act attempts to walk a tightrope between encouraging innovation and preventing risks, but this balance is almost impossible to satisfy everyone. For Coinbase, the act is too restrictive; for traditional banks, it's too lenient; for other crypto companies, it might be just right. The regulatory dilemma lies in its attempt to set boundaries for insatiable desires. Every rule implemented is merely the beginning of the next game. But the most significant cost is that this civil war has shaken the foundations of the crypto industry. What exactly is the crypto industry? Is it a social experiment about decentralization and individual freedom, or a business about asset appreciation and wealth creation? Is it a revolution of the existing financial system, or a supplement and improvement to it? Armstrong's resolute decision, along with the compromises of others in the industry, together paints a picture of the industry's current reality: a paradoxical entity constantly oscillating between idealism and reality, revolution and commerce.

data.title: History of Yellow Card, a Nigerian cryptocurrency company
data.description:
This article, originally from TechCabal and titled "The crypto ban that made us stronger": Day 1-1000 of Yellow Card, is written by Zia Yusuf.

This article tells the story of Chris Maurice, co-founder and CEO of Yellow Card, how he decided to create Yellow Card, how it survived the cryptocurrency ban in Nigeria, and a summary of its development experience.

Yellow Card is currently Nigeria's largest cryptocurrency exchange focused on stablecoins. Its development has been fraught with twists and turns. In the current context of increasing global cryptocurrency centralization, Yellow Card is also seeking new development opportunities.

In 2018, when Chris Morris landed in Lagos, Nigeria's largest city, on a continent far from the United States, he faced two choices: make the "Yellow Card" a success or stay in Nigeria permanently. Day 1 Begins It all started with $90. In Alabama, a state Morris sarcastically referred to as the "Innovation Capital," he met a Nigerian man at Wells Fargo who wanted to wire $200 to his family in Nigeria, but Wells Fargo wanted to charge a $90 fee. “I was thinking, you know, this is crazy, right?” Morris recalled. “How could it possibly cost that much money?” He did what any cryptocurrency enthusiast would do: introduce Bitcoin to the man. “Free transfers! Instant transfers! Revolutionary!” Then he went home, but reality hit him hard. “I went back and thought, what can this man’s mother do with $200 worth of Bitcoin?” Morris said. “It can’t buy food, it can’t pay rent. What problem does it solve?” This question led Morris to delve into Nigeria and its currency, banking system, and economy. During his research, he realized a crucial fact: if he wanted to understand Nigeria, he had to talk to the locals. So, he did something that seemed like a nerdy thing: he posted an ad online—“I want to talk to people in Nigeria,” Morris said, pausing. "Looking back now, I realize my wording might have been a bit off." His words elicited some odd responses (note: you can imagine what they were like). But eventually, he connected with the right Nigerian, and it was then that Morris learned his first lesson about Nigeria: Nigerians are among the most persuasive people in the world. "About a month and a half after meeting this Nigerian man online, he convinced me to get a passport and take my first international flight," Morris said. He had never left the United States. Aside from online searches and perhaps watching a YouTube video or two, he knew almost nothing about Nigeria. But none of that mattered. "To be honest, I only had two choices at the time: either make something of myself or spend the rest of my life in Lagos," Morris said. This story of unwavering founder determination might sound crazy, but when you realize: it worked.

An Unexpected Choice

When Maurice and his co-founder Justin Polux went to Nigeria, they wanted to develop a remittance app. To make it easier for people to send money home. Simple, obvious, yet absolutely necessary.
But that wasn't the case.
“The fact is, there are already 500 remittance apps, right?” Maurice said. “By the time I finish saying that, you might have downloaded 700 different remittance apps, but the world doesn’t need another one.”
What Nigeria, and indeed the entire African continent, needed was something more fundamental: a better way to facilitate international payments and enable funds to interact with local economies.
“Stablecoins are the first and only technology that can truly achieve this,” Maurice explained. “There’s a huge opportunity in this for international payments, dollar access, and solving some other fundamental problems across the continent.”
This realization completely changed everything. Instead of developing another platform to compete with numerous other remittance apps, Yellow Card is focused on building infrastructure—paving the way for all companies to do business across the continent. "Instead of developing our own remittance app, how can we make it easier for all these remittance companies to use our services?" Morris said. Yellow Card launched in Nigeria in 2019. Initially, everything went smoothly. Morris discovered that Nigerians possess a quality lacking in most other markets: a genuine openness to new technologies. "From the beginning, everyone was very knowledgeable about cryptocurrency," he said. "People are very open, eager to try new technologies, and willing to apply them to solve their problems. This is one of the biggest advantages of doing business in Nigeria—a culture of innovation." Morris compared the situation in Nigeria to his experience in Europe. In Europe, the pace of innovation is slower, there are more holidays, and risk-taking is discouraged. In Nigeria, just like in the United States, people are working hard. "There's no boundary between work and life; they're intertwined," he said. “People work incredibly hard in Nigeria. People work incredibly hard in some other countries in the world, right? From a business perspective, those countries have better economies.” By 2021, Nigeria accounted for over 90% of Yellow Card’s total business volume and revenue. The company had built significant infrastructure in seven other African countries, but Nigeria was the engine of its growth. Day 500: The Ban That Separates Winners from Losers Then, in February 2021, everything changed. The Central Bank of Nigeria issued a directive prohibiting banks and financial institutions from processing transactions from cryptocurrency companies or users. Strictly speaking, this wasn’t a full cryptocurrency ban—Nigeria has never truly banned users from holding and using cryptocurrencies—but it effectively served a similar purpose. “You see, this is a huge hindrance to the entire industry and to growth in Nigeria,” Morris said. For most cryptocurrency companies operating in Nigeria, the directive was devastating. Companies that received seed funding around the same time as Yellow Card began laying off staff, experiencing stagnant growth, and some even went bankrupt. Yellow Card, however, did not lay off any employees. "We were the only company that didn't lay off any employees during that crisis," Morris said. "We were also the only company that successfully completed a Series A funding round during that crisis." What was the difference? In the other seven countries. While competitors were fully committed to the Nigerian market, Yellow Card had already established physical presence, obtained bank accounts, acquired licenses, and built infrastructure across the continent. Once Nigeria's network collapsed, Yellow Card could immediately shift resources. "We are the only pan-African cryptocurrency company that has truly built meaningful infrastructure outside of Nigeria," Morris explained. "That's why we are the only company that can shift resources to other countries, other markets, to achieve growth." The ban lasted for about two months, after which everything largely returned to normal. Yellow Card partnered with payment service providers to maintain operations, although from an operational perspective, "the situation was slightly worse." But it caused significant damage to competitors. “This was a huge shock to us and others, making us realize we couldn’t just operate in Nigeria,” Morris said. “Concentrating on one country is too risky.” Yellow Card has actively diversified its business. Today, it operates in 20 African countries. Nigeria remains a large market, “but far from 90%. It doesn’t even account for half of the company’s business.” Of all the cryptocurrency companies that launched around the same time, Yellow Card was the only one to complete a Series A funding round. It has built a sustainable business model. And it was able to do this because it prepared for the worst before it happened. Beyond the Big Three One of Yellow Card’s core assumptions—and one that has proven correct—is that opportunities exist outside of Nigeria, Kenya, and South Africa. “Most companies don’t actually operate in Africa,” Morris said. “They only operate in Nigeria, South Africa, and Kenya, right? Sometimes in Ghana. That’s it. There are 54 countries on the African continent, and they only operate in three or four.” Following Nigeria, Yellow Card’s second launch country was Botswana. “To this day, I think we are still the only company operating locally in Botswana,” Morris said. “Botswana is a huge market for us, like some other markets that people wouldn’t normally think of.” This strategy requires close collaboration with regulators in each country—something previous cryptocurrency companies failed to do effectively. “We weren’t the first company on the African continent to venture into the cryptocurrency space, but we were the first to realize that, as part of it, we needed to help the entire industry grow,” Morris explained. “We work with regulators country by country, developing licensing regimes. Many of the recent bills—we drafted them.” This work isn’t glamorous, and progress is slow, but it’s this work that creates a sustainable business. Day 1000: Data-Driven Rapid Trial and Error
Morris is unabashed about mistakes.
Yellow Card has made mistakes in marketing, product decisions, and operational choices. But it has also gradually developed a philosophy: don't make mistakes for long.
“You can go in, you can make assumptions, you might make mistakes, but don't make mistakes for long,” he says. “Trial and error quickly, fail quickly.”
This is a philosophy stemming from practical needs. Operating in 20 African countries means dealing with 20 different regulatory environments, 20 different user cultures, and 20 different market dynamics; you can't afford to be wrong.
“We try to be as data-driven as possible,” Morris says. “Beyond the basic business model—we're going to operate in all these countries, and there are opportunities in all of them—we don't make any long-term assumptions.”
This is also why Morris believes the African cryptocurrency market is severely underestimated. He said, “Africa is the leading continent in this field by many metrics. Seven countries rank among the top or fluctuate in global cryptocurrency adoption rates. It’s a huge market that I think is largely overlooked.” Why this oversight? Morris points to a lack of representativeness in external communication. He said, “Africa receives very little media coverage. It’s barely represented in popular culture, film, and other areas. People outside the continent generally don’t know much about it.” Even within Africa, there are differences in understanding. “If you talk to an average person in Nigeria, they don’t really understand French-speaking Africa and some of its cultural nuances, and vice versa.” In contrast, American or British culture dominates global media. “I can’t think of a single film set in Congo,” Morris said. “Unless you specifically do research, it’s difficult to learn more.” Next: Looking to the Long Term Yellow Card currently operates in 20 African countries, has completed its Series A funding round, and has positioned itself as the continent’s crypto infrastructure. In 2025, Yellow Card discontinued its retail services and focused on corporate clients. New banking restrictions on cryptocurrencies in Nigeria in 2023 also created new opportunities. What advice does Morris give to entrepreneurs? Keep an open mind. “I didn’t experience any major ideological clashes in Nigeria because I went there without any preconceived notions,” he said. “I think experiencing a clash of ideologies is about having certain expectations about something, only to find that the outcome is completely different. I had no idea what I was going to encounter.” This open-minded attitude, coupled with proactive diversification, regulatory cooperation, and a willingness to quickly learn from mistakes, transformed a one-way gamble into a pan-African success story. Looking back, Maurice's assessment of his initial decision to fly to Lagos with a brand-new passport valid for only six days is simple: “Either work hard, or spend the rest of your life in Lagos.” He has built an effective system that spans more than 20 countries and continues to expand.

data.title: AI Industry Insights: Exploring Subjective Initiative Beyond the Boundaries of AI Capabilities
data.description:
I've seen many friends showing off their new AI tools, demonstrating their prompting skills, and sharing dazzling workflows. But, but, I wonder if everyone shares the same feeling: we, the "smart people" who think we're at the forefront of the trend, are actually just struggling to catch up. AI is developing so fast that even immersing ourselves in it all feels like we can't keep up:

1) If we were to define the "half-life" of learning AI skills, it would probably be measured in weeks.

I was just learning how to better control Cursor, and then Claude Code came out and became a sensation. I was just proud of the various prompting engineering skills I figured out, but after Skills came out, I suddenly felt that those skills were no longer useful... In the past, learning a skill could last at least three to five years, but now it might be outdated in three to five months.

This is the harsh reality: the skills and techniques you spend countless hours developing may not keep up with a single new version of AI. But you'll gradually realize that AI will eventually level the playing field for everyone. Whose tools are unique, whose prompts are more sophisticated—all these factors will be eliminated. What will ultimately determine the competition? "Curiosity and learning ability." While others still think AI tools are irrelevant to them, your repeated exploration, experience, and trial and error will have already put you ahead. 2) Using AI has gone from being done clandestinely to being a source of pride. Furthermore, I've observed a very interesting phenomenon: six months ago, everyone used AI to write code secretly, afraid of being discovered that "your code was purely generated by AI." Now? My programmer friends have started proudly showing off their AI-powered projects, saying things like, "Look at this dashboard, look at this little app I made, Claude finished it in 10 minutes this morning." This shift in mindset is crucial. In the past, our professional value was based on "what skills I have," but now it's shifting to "what I can accomplish with AI." Just like after the Industrial Revolution, no one laughed at you for using machines instead of handcrafting; AI is the same—it's a productivity tool. Those who reject AI will eventually find that it's not AI that eliminates you, but those who use AI. Speed ​​itself is a barrier. 3) For those working with AI, explore the subjective initiative beyond its boundaries. Of course, this doesn't mean we should blindly rely on AI. Often, AI will overstep its bounds, operating outside your intended scope and wasting a lot of valuable time. This forces us to manage AI with cognitive logic, rather than being completely led by it. It's important to understand that even the most powerful AI is just a tool; it can't provide you with the understanding of "what to do" and "why to do it." For example, you might simply want AI to optimize a simple data query function, but it unnecessarily restructures your database architecture. AI execution faces significant challenges in terms of triggering conditions and defining rules. These are areas where we should expand our capabilities, thinking beyond what AI can do, especially considering its path-dependent limitations. Then, we can leverage human subjectivity. Ultimately, managing AI isn't about chasing the pace of AI tool iterations, but about truly understanding the limitations of AI's thinking and execution, and then using the innate wisdom of "consciousness" to fill those gaps.

data.title: West Virginia lawmaker introduces bill to allow state treasury crypto investments
data.description:
West Virginia Eyes Bitcoin for State Treasury in Bold Inflation Hedge Push

West Virginia may soon join a small but growing group of U.S. states experimenting with crypto-backed public finances. State Senator Chris Rose has introduced new legislation that would authorize the state treasury to invest directly in Bitcoin, approved stablecoins, and precious metals, framing digital assets as a potential shield against inflation and monetary instability.

The bill, formally titled the Inflation Protection Act, would amend West Virginia’s investment code to expand the types of assets the Board of Treasury is permitted to hold. Under the proposal, the state could allocate capital to precious metals as well as digital assets that recorded a market capitalization of more than $750 billion in the previous calendar year. As of January, that threshold applies to Bitcoin alone, effectively positioning the legislation as a pathway to a state-level Bitcoin reserve.

If passed, the bill would mark a significant shift in how state governments approach digital assets, moving crypto from the fringes of public finance into formally sanctioned reserve strategies.

The legislation also lays out strict guardrails for how any crypto exposure would be managed. Digital assets held by the state would need to be stored with a qualified custodian, accessed through exchange-traded products, or secured using institutional-grade custody solutions designed to meet public-sector risk standards.

Stablecoins, meanwhile, would face even tighter scrutiny, requiring explicit regulatory approval from either federal authorities or individual U.S. states before they could be added to the treasury’s balance sheet.

These custody and compliance requirements appear designed to preempt common criticisms of government crypto holdings, particularly around security, transparency, and regulatory risk, while aligning with emerging federal frameworks governing payment stablecoins.

West Virginia’s proposal mirrors a broader national trend. In 2025, Texas and Arizona moved forward with legislation enabling Bitcoin reserve frameworks, while New Hampshire authorized state-level crypto holdings through separate measures.

Although dozens of similar bills have been introduced across the U.S. over the past two years, only a handful have successfully cleared the legislative process, underscoring how politically sensitive public crypto adoption remains.

The West Virginia bill has now been referred to the Committee on Banking and Insurance, where its prospects remain uncertain. Still, its introduction alone adds momentum to the state-level crypto reserve conversation.

JUST IN: 🇺🇸 Arizona senator proposes exempting Bitcoin and crypto from taxes

Arizona is already one of just 3 U.S. states with a Bitcoin strategic reserve 🔥 pic.twitter.com/JkrsXvb4UZ

— Bitcoin Archive (@BitcoinArchive) December 23, 2025

Notably, the move comes as federal lawmakers continue to struggle with digital asset policy. In Washington, U.S. Senators recently delayed markup of the CLARITY Act, a sweeping proposal aimed at defining market structure for cryptocurrencies.

The bill has faced pushback from industry participants over its treatment of decentralized finance, stablecoin rewards, and the balance of power between the SEC, the CFTC, and banking regulators. Against that backdrop of federal gridlock, states appear increasingly willing to chart their own paths.

Supporters argue that Bitcoin’s fixed supply and growing institutional adoption make it an attractive non-correlated reserve asset, comparable to gold in an era of rising debt and currency debasement. Critics, however, warn that volatility and political optics could complicate public-sector exposure to crypto, even with high market-cap thresholds in place.

Still, by setting the bar at $750 billion, West Virginia’s proposal deliberately excludes speculative altcoins, limiting eligibility to the most liquid and established digital assets. Whether framed as a strategic inflation hedge or a symbolic political statement, the bill reflects a clear shift: crypto is no longer just a private-sector experiment — it’s increasingly a public finance debate.

data.title: Google Play Will Remove Unregistered Foreign Crypto Apps From South Korea From 28 January
data.description:
Google Play Will Block Unregistered Crypto Apps In South Korea

South Korean Android users will soon lose access to most foreign cryptocurrency exchange apps on Google Play, as new rules now require all platforms to hold local regulatory approval.

Google’s updated “Crypto Exchange and Software Wallet Policy” mandates that apps must be registered as a Virtual Asset Service Provider (VASP) with the Korea Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) to remain listed or receive updates.

Which Exchanges Will Be Affected And Why

The change, effective from 28 January, will primarily affect global exchanges such as Binance, Bybit, KuCoin, OKX, and MEXC, none of which have completed the Korean VASP registration process.

🚨GOOGLE PLAY STORE TO BAN OVERSEAS CRYPTO APPS IN S. KOREA

From Jan. 28, Google Play Store will require crypto apps to register as VASPs with Korean authorities.

This will block Android access to overseas exchanges as compliance is nearly impossible for foreign firms. pic.twitter.com/4PKGl9vo7b

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) January 16, 2026

Only 27 domestic platforms, including major exchanges Upbit and Bithumb, have secured approval so far.

Registration is considered highly complex for overseas exchanges, requiring strict anti-money laundering measures and local security certifications, which many foreign platforms are unable to meet.

2026년 기준 가상자산사업자(VASP) 및 서비스 명단

해외거래소 차단을 어디까지 할 지 모르겠지만 정부의 은혜로
코리안들은 이제 이놈들 위주로 써야함 ㄷㄷ

업비트 빗썸 말고는 참 안쓰고 싶게 생긴 스캠 느낌 나는 이름뿐임 ㄷㄷ pic.twitter.com/S3aO65vlgG

— BioMan🪙 (@ganziboy11) January 15, 2026

Can Users Still Access These Platforms

Despite app restrictions, users can still reach foreign exchanges through mobile or desktop web browsers.

Some community members have also discussed sideloading APK files or using VPNs to bypass Play Store limitations.

Local outlet Digital Asset noted that “these workarounds significantly increase security risks for users,” leaving traders exposed to potential malware, phishing attacks, and compromised applications.

Impact On Korean Retail Investors And Trading

South Korea hosts one of the world’s largest crypto markets, with over 10 million active users — around 20% of the population.

According to the Financial Services Commission, the country’s crypto market was valued at 95 trillion won ($64.6 billion) as of June 2025, with an average daily trading volume of $4.35 billion.

News1 reported that many Korean retail investors rely on overseas platforms for higher leverage, arbitrage opportunities, and access to a wider range of tokens, meaning the Play Store ban could reshape trading patterns.

🇰🇷$110 BILLION LEFT KOREAN EXCHANGES

In 2025, around $110B in crypto moved to wallets and offshore platforms as local rules limited retail investors.

Korean traders generated $3.36B in fees overseas with Binance alone capturing 58% as per Tiger Research. pic.twitter.com/VKfM2nQZ9Q

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) January 5, 2026

Will Local Exchanges Dominate The Market

With foreign apps effectively sidelined, domestic exchanges like Upbit and Bithumb are expected to consolidate control over trading volume, token listings, and fees.

This could limit asset diversity and slow competitive innovation in the local market, though some traders may pivot to decentralized finance platforms or non-custodial wallets, which are not restricted by app store regulations.

How Global Exchanges Might Respond

Some international platforms may explore partnerships or stakes in VASP-licensed Korean firms, similar to Binance’s previous collaboration with Gopax.

Binance acquired Gopax, and Bybit is looking to acquire Korbit.

Both rank around #4-5 in Korea, with volumes far below Upbit & Bithumb.

But with Binance & Bybit flexing their global reach, there’s serious growth potential.

You need to recognize that Korea has become a key… pic.twitter.com/mbDZlHqRHb

— Min l Hyperithm (@ingerxyz) November 10, 2025

Even with local compliance, offerings could remain limited, particularly for crypto derivatives, which are prohibited under Korean rules.

Could Restrictions Expand Beyond Google Play

South Korea’s authorities have already limited unregistered platforms from providing Korean-language support, marketing to local users, or handling Korean won transactions.

While the current policy only affects Play Store apps, experts suggest that similar restrictions could eventually apply to web access or the Apple App Store, further shaping how Koreans trade crypto internationally.

data.title: What does Binance's 133-page annual report say?
data.description:
Binance Research just released its annual report, "FULL YEAR 2025 & THEMES FOR 2026," which not only provides a comprehensive review of 2025 but also forecasts industry development themes for 2026.

The report covers a wide range of topics in the crypto market, including macroeconomics, Bitcoin, protocol layers, DeFi, stablecoins, consumer crypto, cutting-edge technologies, institutional adoption, regulatory policies, and themes for 2026.

Web3.0 Study Room has helped summarize the core content of this 133-page report, which may help you find your next wealth direction.

2025: A Year of Contradictions

(Image: Major Events of 2025)

If you had to summarize the crypto market in 2025 in one sentence, it would be: "Milestones and volatility coexist."

If you had to summarize the crypto market in 2025 in one sentence, it would be: "Milestones and volatility coexist."

On the one hand, tangible progress has been made in infrastructure and institutional acceptance. Bitcoin ETFs continue to attract funds, with net inflows exceeding $21.3 billion in the US alone. Products from asset management giants like BlackRock even surpass the size of the largest gold ETF. Stablecoins have gained mainstream acceptance, with annual trading volume soaring to $33 trillion, surpassing traditional payment giants like Visa to become a new backbone of global payments. Nearly 200 listed companies have included Bitcoin in their balance sheets, with total holdings exceeding 1.1 million coins, representing 5.5% of the total Bitcoin supply. However, the market remains quite volatile. While the global cryptocurrency market capitalization reached a peak of $4.2 trillion for the first time, with BTC hitting a record high of $126,000, the market ultimately declined by 7.9% at the end of the year, with the total market capitalization hovering around $3.35 trillion. Behind these dramatic ups and downs lies the strong dominance of macroeconomic factors. Is the crypto market "out of control"? It is no longer an independent sector. In the past, we looked at halvings and technological upgrades. Now, market pricing power has shifted to the Federal Reserve's interest rates, US fiscal bills, and geopolitical conflicts. The report points out that the correlation between Bitcoin and US stocks surged to 0.43 in 2025, and during the US government shutdown and data vacuum, the crypto market experienced a rare "decoupling" and decline compared to traditional assets. This reveals a key signal: the price of crypto assets is increasingly influenced by traditional macroeconomic and financial cycles, and is no longer merely a matter of technical analysis or narrative within the crypto space. In other words, cryptocurrencies are "matureing," needing to both enjoy the glory of institutional investment and endure the growing pains brought about by global economic fluctuations. Those who used to make quick money through hype and narratives now need to understand government bond yields and fiscal deficits. Trend 1: "Risk Reboot" in 2026 The report believes that the biggest driver of the market in 2026 may come from policy. In summary: fiscal stimulus, monetary easing, and regulatory relaxation will jointly inject liquidity into the crypto market. First, fiscal stimulus. Fiscal stimulus, exemplified by the US "OBBBA" bill (such as tax rebates and corporate investment incentives), is expected to inject a large amount of cash into the economy in the first quarter of 2026. Historical experience shows that cash flowing directly into households tends to flow more quickly into high-risk assets (such as meme coins and NFTs). Secondly, there's monetary easing. The Federal Reserve is expected to continue cutting interest rates, potentially lowering the policy rate to around 3%, while simultaneously restarting its asset purchase program, injecting an estimated $500-600 billion in liquidity throughout the year. Central banks in Europe and other countries are also expected to shift towards interest rate cuts and balance sheet expansion, providing more ample liquidity to the market. For crypto assets, low-cost funding means more funds will flow to high-risk, high-return markets. Finally, there's regulatory clarity. In addition to the stablecoin regulatory framework (the US GENIUS Act established the first federal-level regulatory framework for stablecoins, and the EU MiCA regulation is already in effect), the US Clarity Act is expected to pass in 2026, clarifying the regulatory scope of crypto assets and reducing industry uncertainty. The clearer the compliance path, the more it will alleviate the concerns of traditional institutions and attract "regular players" to enter the market. What does this mean for ordinary people? A more relaxed monetary environment and clearer rules of the game may shift market sentiment from the "data fog" of 2025 to a "risk restart." However, it should be noted that this does not mean a one-sided rise, but rather that the market structure may shift from being driven more by retail investors to being dominated by institutions and sovereign liquidity. Trend Two: Stablecoins Moving Towards Financial Infrastructure In 2025, stablecoins (crypto assets pegged to fiat currencies) experienced their "golden age." Their market capitalization surged nearly 50% throughout the year, exceeding $305 billion, with daily trading volume reaching $3.54 trillion. This figure is 2.6 times that of Visa, meaning that a massive amount of funds flow through stablecoins globally every day.

Among them, Ethena's USDe performed the best, becoming the third largest stablecoin at one point through government bond collateral and hedging strategies, with a peak market capitalization of $14.8 billion.

... If 2025 was the "breakout year" for stablecoins, then 2026 may be the "application year" for their integration into daily life. The report points out that the next stage of growth for stablecoins will be deeply integrated with Neobank applications. These blockchain-based applications, with their lower operating costs, can provide users with higher savings returns than traditional banks. More importantly, payment giants like PayPal and Stripe, as well as card organizations like Visa and Mastercard, are actively embracing stablecoins and integrating them into their products. Stablecoins are no longer just "intermediary tools" for crypto transactions; they are beginning to penetrate real-world scenarios such as cross-border payments, corporate settlements, and personal savings. Trend Three: Institutions' Attitudes Towards Cryptocurrencies are Undergoing a Fundamental Shift: From Simple Price Speculation to Embedding Blockchain Technology into Core Financial Workflows. The most obvious signal is the booming US spot BTC ETF – with a net inflow of over $21.3 billion throughout the year. BlackRock's IBIT ETF alone attracted $25 billion, far exceeding other similar products. More importantly, institutional funds are no longer limited to BTC but have extended into the RWA (Real-World Assets, which are tokenized versions of traditional assets such as government bonds and stocks). In 2025, the total value locked (TVL) of RWA surpassed $17 billion, exceeding DEX (Decentralized Exchanges) for the first time to become the fifth largest category in the DeFi field.

1. As collateral: Top banks such as JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America have begun pilot programs accepting Bitcoin as loan collateral. This means that cryptocurrencies are being recognized as "financial-grade" assets.

1. As collateral:

2. **Issuing Tokenized Funds:** Asset management giants such as BlackRock and Franklin Templeton have already issued tokenized money market funds (MMFs) on the blockchain. This allows fund shares to be traded and transferred almost in real time and used more flexibly as collateral in DeFi. 3. **Building Hybrid Data Sources:** Traditional financial data giants (such as ICE) are collaborating with blockchain oracles (such as Chainlink) to "put" data such as foreign exchange and commodities "on-chain," supporting more complex on-chain financial products. The deeper implication of this trend is that in the future crypto market, value will not only come from asset appreciation, but also from the demand for blockchain as an efficient and programmable financial infrastructure. Institutions bring not only capital, but also real-world application scenarios and liquidity. An important warning: Beware of the "digital bond" bubble. Amid the frenzy of listed companies buying Bitcoin, the report also issued a sobering warning: We need to be wary of the business model risks of some "digital asset bond companies." As of the end of 2025, 194 listed companies held over 1.1 million BTC, accounting for 5.5% of the total supply. Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) alone held 672,500 BTC, making it the largest corporate BTC holder. These companies primarily engage in high-leverage accumulating of Bitcoin through bond issuance or financing. In a bull market, their stock price increases may far exceed those of Bitcoin itself. However, once the market corrects, they face **double pressure**: their stock price declines may be far greater than the decline in Bitcoin's price. Rising financing costs or closed channels may force companies to sell assets to maintain operations. In the second half of 2025, some such companies' stock prices traded at a significant discount relative to their net asset value (NAV) in Bitcoin, reflecting market concerns. The report cautions that investors should focus on entities with moderate leverage and long-term cash flow plans, rather than "castles in the air" that rely solely on asset price appreciation. Looking ahead to 2026, how should we proceed? The report's implied recommendations are clear for different participants. 1. For ordinary investors: Understand that the market has entered a deeper "institutionalized" phase. There's no need to blindly chase niche assets; focus on two types of opportunities: Compliant leading crypto asset ETFs (especially BTC and ETH-related products). Stablecoins and RWA products with real-world applications offer lower risk and higher certainty. 2. For industry builders: Infrastructure is relatively mature; institutional services, RWA infrastructure, and cross-chain (interconnectivity between different blockchains) will be the three major growth areas. As the integration of traditional finance and the crypto market deepens, projects that can solve compliance, liquidity, and efficiency issues are more likely to attract capital. 3. For Observers: Focus less on price charts and more on seemingly "boring" indicators: stablecoin adoption rates, on-chain settlement volumes of multinational corporations, and the scale of tokenization of mainstream financial products. These are the real signals of whether the industry's foundation is solid. The crypto world is shedding its early hype and mystery. "Blockchain is dead, long live blockchain." The technology itself will become invisible, while the "verifiable trust" it brings will permeate our lives. Appendix: Other Key Information Mentioned in the Report Bitcoin's market capitalization remained at $1.8 trillion, with a hash rate exceeding 1 ZH/s, mining difficulty increasing by 36.4% year-over-year, and on-chain activity slowing. Liquidity and demand shifted to off-chain channels (ETFs, institutional custody). ETH, Solana, and BNB Chain dominated the market. Among them: Ethereum: Leading in developers, DeFi liquidity, and total value, but the ETH/BTC exchange rate is weakening. SOL: Daily trading volume exceeds 100 million transactions, with a total daily trading volume exceeding 13.5 billion transactions, becoming the third crypto asset to be approved for a US spot ETF. BNB Chain: DEX trading volume increased by 164%, attracting institutions such as BlackRock and VanEck to issue tokenized assets.

DeFi

  • TVL reached $124.4 billion, with funds shifting towards stablecoins and yield assets.

  • DEX/CEX spot trading ratio reached 20%, with significant growth in derivatives trading.

  • Prediction markets are on the rise, with Polymarket and Kalshi reaching $51 billion in trading volume.

Consumer-grade crypto applications

Wallets become killer applications, MetaMask and Phantom lead in user numbers, and CEX user scale continues to grow; Social and gaming sectors cool down, Farcaster shifts to wallet development; NFT market value drops by 69%, blockchain game financing cools down.

Cutting-edge technology

  • x402 protocol: supports AI agent payments, with cumulative transaction volume exceeding $30 million.

On-chain privacy: Demand for privacy coins like Zcash is rising, and compliant privacy solutions are developing. Furthermore, the rise of DePAI (Decentralized Physical Artificial Intelligence) is driving the integration of the machine economy with blockchain.
